topic: A person's online friend turns out to live in the same city
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, a cheerful little ping from 'PixelDreamer69.' I stared at the screen, my jaw tightening until my molars ached. It was a picture of a coffee shop I recognized instantly, the one near the old library. A small, almost imperceptible detail caught my eye: the subtle angle of the window reflection. It was the same angle I’d seen on a street corner near my apartment building this morning. A cold knot formed in my stomach, tightening with every second of recognition. I quickly minimized the chat window, my fingers hovering over the 'block' button, a strange, unpleasant heat rising in my cheeks. How could someone I’d only ever known through a glowing screen be breathing the same air as me?

[story 2]

"Seriously? You live *here*?" The words tasted like ash in my mouth. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. My hands started to tremble, a shallow, rapid vibration I couldn't control. I re-read the profile, the carefully curated aesthetic suddenly feeling like a grotesque performance. I found a shared landmark—a specific, slightly dilapidated park bench—and the realization hit me like a physical blow. I scrubbed my eyes with the heel of my hand, feeling a profound sense of invasion. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my living room, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. This felt like a violation, a cheap trick played on my sense of reality.

[story 3]

I watched the map application, my breath catching in my throat. The pin dropped precisely where I knew the old bakery was—the one with the perpetually sticky floor. My knuckles were white where I gripped my phone. A slow, heavy wave of unease washed over me, making my skin feel clammy. I scrolled through the recent photos, searching for any corroboration, any sign that this was some elaborate setup. Then I saw it: a blurred background in one of the selfies, unmistakably the skyline visible from my window at dusk. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped me, ragged and shallow. The air in the room suddenly felt too thick, too heavy to draw in. I felt a distinct urge to throw the device across the room, to shatter the illusion.
